title: loong64 (LoongArch64) port: working bare-metal PoC, seeking guidance to land it fully
author: tannevaled
body:
Hi — I'd like to contribute **`GOOS=tamago GOARCH=loong64`** and have a working proof-of-concept I'd like to align on before pushing toward a complete, upstreamable port.

## What works today (verified on QEMU `-M virt`)

A loong64-patched `tamago-go` + a `loong64` framework package boot a pure-Go program bare-metal and run a **functional Go runtime**:

- boots; **GC/allocator, goroutines + channels, maps, slice growth** all work
- **monotonic `Nanotime`** via the stable timer (`RDTIMED`) + `CPUCFG` frequency
- **RNG** via a timer-seeded PRNG (placeholder for a real entropy source)
- **exception handling**: `CSR.EENTRY` → a handler that reports faults instead of the silent jump-to-0 (verified with a deliberate bad-address read)

Demo output:
```
tamago/loong64: runtime up
monotonic clock: busy-loop took ~ 2529 us
map[5]: 25
PRNG samples (should differ): 41920 57918
goroutine workers total: 150500
tamago/loong64: DONE
triggering a test fault (expect EXC)... EXC
```

## How it's structured (mirrors the riscv64 port)

- **tamago-go fork (small):** `tamago/loong64` added to the GOOS/GOARCH allowlist (`cmd/dist/build.go`, `internal/platform`), `cmd/link/.../loong64` accepts `Htamago`, `os_tamago_loong64.go`, `rt0_tamago_loong64.s`, `sys_tamago_loong64.s` (rt0 bring-up + `GetG` + stubbed `Wake`), `cpu_loong64_other.go` (osInit stub). Upstream Go already ships loong64 codegen + base runtime, so this is glue, not a backend.
- **tamago framework:** `goos/goos_loong64.s` + a `loong64/` package (`Init`/`cpuinit` bring-up — FPU enable via WORD-encoded `csrwr EUEN`, SP setup, `_rt0_tamago_start`; stable-timer counter; minimal exception entry).

Patches available; happy to open a PR in whatever shape you prefer.

## Where I'd like your design guidance (the remaining pieces)

These are coupled to tamago internals / the toolchain, so I want to match your architecture rather than guess:

1. **Timer-driven preemption / IRQ.** riscv64's `trapHandler` jumps to `handleInterrupt` / `systemException` (the runtime's IRQ→`SIGTRAP` signal path). What's the intended way to wire a new arch into that — which runtime-side hooks must a loong64 trap handler provide, and how should context save/restore + `ERTN` integrate with the signal/preempt machinery?
2. **`-buildmode=pie`.** Not currently enabled for `tamago/loong64`; it's needed for relocatable images (e.g. UEFI). Is enabling gc PIE for a bare-metal GOOS a matter of the buildmode allowlist + linker path, or are there tamago-specific considerations?
3. **DMW + paging.** For >direct-range RAM and proper cache attributes — any preference on the bring-up sequence (DMW0/1 windows + `CRMD.PG`) and the link layout vs. the current DA-mode PoC?
4. **UEFI** (longer-term): a loong64 `uefi` board analogous to go-boot — happy to drive this once the core lands.
5. **Naming/structure**: package name `loong64`, CSR-op convention (Go's loong64 assembler has no `csr*` mnemonic — I WORD-encode; would you prefer adding the mnemonics?), and how you'd like the PoC split into reviewable PRs.

I've signed the CLA and will work via your normal review flow. Is loong64 wanted, and is anyone already on it? Happy to do the work — just want it shaped to land.

title: New SoC upstreaming
author: MDr164
body:
Hi Andrea,
I'm currently working on a couple new ports (2 active and almost done just need to add some more peripherals, 3 more lined up afterwards) that I would like to upstream here as well. I currently got a couple question around the logistics there:
1. Is there already a preferred way how to structure a HAL for different peripheral interfaces like SPI, I2C, etc shared between ports?
2. I got a platform that uses some SiFive IPs in silicon but is not a SiFive based system, is it fine for platforms like these to just import from other ports or should items like that maybe made common somehow?
3. One of the platforms has a pretty extensive 32GBit l2 switch peripheral, I could upstream that one here as well but that part is specific and therefore no really reusable, I assume it should be used just within the SoC. Any opinion on 'internal' packages for stuff like that or should I just place it directly into the soc folder to match other platforms structure?

I can also open up draft PRs for each SoC under which we could discuss in more detail but I assume things like where to place common driver interfaces would affect both anyways (I'm currently duplicating a bit of code there)

title: Proposal: Export CAAM.job
author: karlo195

title: Nil pointer handling in RISCV target
author: marcinbugaj
body:
I can't find a place in the TamaGo sources where nil pointers are handled. Are nil pointers handled for that target?

By nil handling I mean recovering from a nil pointer dereference (like [here](https://www.practical-go-lessons.com/post/how-to-recover-from-a-runtime-failure-panic-in-go-cbl28ojpaeds70g0qrs0)) or just panicking in case of nil pointer dereference.

title: qemu amd64 hello world build error
author: bradfitz
body:
Hi, am I holding this wrong?

Using Tamago 1.25.1 ...

```
% ~/sdk/tgo/go version
go version go1.25.1 darwin/arm64
% ~/sdk/tgo/go tool dist list | grep tama
tamago/amd64
tamago/arm
tamago/arm64
tamago/riscv64
```

With a hello world project...

```
% cat go.mod 
module tamago

go 1.25.0

require github.com/usbarmory/tamago v1.25.1 // indirect

% cat tamago.go                          
package main

import (
        _ "github.com/usbarmory/tamago/board/qemu/microvm"
)

func main() {
        println("hello world")
}
```

It fails to build:

```
% make
GOOS=tamago GOARCH=amd64 /Users/bradfitz/sdk/tgo/go build -ldflags "-T 0x10010000 -R 0x1000" .
# github.com/usbarmory/tamago/amd64
../../go/pkg/mod/github.com/usbarmory/tamago@v1.25.1/amd64/irq.go:148:19: cannot use runtime.GetG() (value of type uint) as uint64 value in assignment
make: *** [build] Error 1
```

I haven't started to dig into git history to figure out if something was changed recently API-wise.

Thanks!

title: question: differences with gokrazy?
author: nlepage
body:
Hi,
I was wondering what are the differences between [gokrazy](https://github.com/gokrazy/gokrazy) and tamago.
Would it be possible (if it is relevant) to have a new entry in ["How does TamaGo differ from past/current similar efforts?" chapter of the FAQ](https://github.com/usbarmory/tamago/wiki/Frequently-Asked-Questions-(FAQ)#how-does-tamago-differ-from-pastcurrent-similar-efforts)?
Thanks in advance for your response!

title: tamago-go compilation fails on macOs
author: cibomahto
body:
Please let me know if this should be reported elsewhere. The tamago-go repository appears to have issues disabled.

When attempting to build tamago-go-1.23.5 on macOs, using the 'official' go binary for bootstrapping, the build succeeds but the package tests fail:

```
‌/all.bash
Building Go cmd/dist using /Users/matt/Downloads/go. (go1.23.5 darwin/amd64)
Building Go toolchain1 using /Users/matt/Downloads/go.
Building Go bootstrap cmd/go (go_bootstrap) using Go toolchain1.
Building Go toolchain2 using go_bootstrap and Go toolchain1.
Building Go toolchain3 using go_bootstrap and Go toolchain2.
Building packages and commands for darwin/amd64.

##### Test execution environment.
# GOARCH: amd64
# CPU: Intel(R) Core(TM) i5-8259U CPU @ 2.30GHz
# GOOS: darwin
# OS Version: Darwin 24.2.0 Darwin Kernel Version 24.2.0: Fri Dec  6 18:41:43 PST 2024; root:xnu-11215.61.5~2/RELEASE_X86_64 x86_64

##### Testing packages.

(snip)

ok  	cmd/distpack	4.733s
ok  	cmd/doc	5.129s
ok  	cmd/fix	14.387s
vcs-test.golang.org rerouted to http://127.0.0.1:63939
https://vcs-test.golang.org rerouted to https://127.0.0.1:63940
go test proxy running at GOPROXY=http://127.0.0.1:63941/mod
--- FAIL: TestScript (5.22s)
    --- FAIL: TestScript/list_testdata (0.05s)
        script_test.go:135: 2025-01-26T19:47:13Z
        script_test.go:137: $WORK=/var/folders/ny/kv7pdbp14893y9qpsb7vx0h40000gn/T/cmd-go-test-4124991580/tmpdir2649195296/list_testdata1288743131
        script_test.go:159:
            # Issue 65406. The testdata directory in GOROOT/src
            # shouldn't be treated as a standard package.
            # disabled to allow GOOS=tamago testing (0.035s)
            > [GOOS:linux] skip
            [condition not met]
            > go list -f '{{.ImportPath}} {{.Dir}}' testdata
            [stderr]
            ambiguous import: found package testdata in multiple modules:
            	testdata (/var/folders/ny/kv7pdbp14893y9qpsb7vx0h40000gn/T/cmd-go-test-4124991580/tmpdir2649195296/list_testdata1288743131/gopath/src)
            	 (/Users/matt/repos/tamamgo/tamago-go/src/testdata)
        script_test.go:159: FAIL: testdata/script/list_testdata.txt:7: go list -f '{{.ImportPath}} {{.Dir}}' testdata: exit status 1
FAIL
FAIL	cmd/go	136.624s
ok  	cmd/go/internal/auth	11.715s
ok  	cmd/go/internal/cache	13.252s

(snip)

ok  	cmd/relnote	10.212s
ok  	cmd/trace	5.173s
ok  	cmd/vet	6.436s
FAIL
go tool dist: Failed: exit status 1
```

Looking at the [offending test](https://github.com/usbarmory/tamago-go/blob/tamago1.23.5/src/cmd/go/testdata/script/list_testdata.txt#L5), there is a skip directive for linux. If I add:

```
[GOOS:darwin] skip
```

then the test run does succeed, but I'm not familiar enough with how this works to say if it is the correct fix.

title: USB Host mode support
author: fabled
body:
I was just able to complete a custom USB device mode support on Armory MK2.

Now I am looking to attach other peripherals to the USB-C receptacle and to communicate to those devices. Also interested in having USB passthrough (that is proxy the traffic between the computer on the USB-C plug and the peripheral connected to USB-C receptacle).

I did not see yet any host controller support code, or did I just not find it? Is support for this planned?

title: Device invariably "dies" after some time
author: marctrem
body:
Dear Tamago authors,

I cannot get the device to live further than a minute on tamago.
The issue has been reproduced with tamago-go#1.18.1 and tamaga-go#1.18.3 and the latest tamago.
The bootloader is loaded using SDP.
Since I lack the debugging hardware, it's rather hard to figure the root cause. No panic is emitted.
The issue is observed with and without the sd card being inserted.

## Repro
### Code
```go
func init() {
	model := imx6.Model()
	if !imx6.Native {
		banner += fmt.Sprintf(" - %s %d MHz (emulated)", model, imx6.ARMFreq()/1000000)
		return
	}
	if err := imx6.SetARMFreq(900); err != nil {
		log.Printf("WARNING: error setting ARM frequency: %v", err)
	}
}

func main() {
	select {}
}

```

### Steps
1. Plug the device
2. Flash through SDP
3. Blue led goes from soft to hard
4. Wait ~34s
5. Blue led goes from hard to soft -- device is unresponsive

## Video
https://drive.google.com/file/d/1_ianaqZ977cbfOYE3gYm8hQSo8weLGW5/view

Please let me know if I can help with that investigation.

Thank you!

title: what does WORD mean in rt0_tamago_arm.s?
author: kekemuyu
body:
### Is "WORD $0xe10f0000" represent a machine code?
runtime/rt0_tamago_arm.s   

```go
TEXT _rt0_arm_tamago(SB),NOSPLIT,$0
	// Detect HYP mode and switch to SVC if necessary
	WORD	$0xe10f0000	// mrs r0, CPSR
	AND	$0x1f, R0, R0	// get processor mode

	CMP	$0x10, R0	// USR mode
	B.EQ	runtime_start	// Skip initialization if USER mode

	CMP	$0x1a, R0	// HYP mode
	B.NE	after_eret	// Skip ERET if not HYP mode

	BIC	$0x1f, R0
	ORR	$0x1d3, R0	// AIF masked, SVC mode
	MOVW	$12(R15), R14	// add lr, pc, #12 (after_eret)
	WORD	$0xe16ff000	// msr SPSR_fsxc, r0
	WORD	$0xe12ef30e	// msr ELR_hyp, lr
	WORD	$0xe160006e	// eret0
```

author: andig
body:
See https://gokrazy.org/. Both seem to provide bare metal, both are lacking Wifi support. gokrazy works with the stock compiler. 
I have some experience with gokrazy- why would I need tamago and its changes to the runtime?

refs https://github.com/golang/go/issues/46802

title: UART initialization
author: saper
body:
I was reading the UART code and I have few questions:

1. Clock frequency: Page 630 of IMX6ULLRM seems to suggest that only `PLL3_FREQ` gets divided by 6; if running on oscillator frequency this should not be done, but https://github.com/f-secure-foundry/tamago/blob/60c33cfb0e60b29dda937a35cd11acd7a44acd42/soc/imx6/uart.go#L281 does it for both cases.
2. p.3603 suggests one should wait for UTS bit 0 to become 0 to wait for the reset to complete.  https://github.com/f-secure-foundry/tamago/blob/60c33cfb0e60b29dda937a35cd11acd7a44acd42/soc/imx6/uart.go#L234 waits on UCR2 bit 0 instead. Is this better for some reason?

3. https://github.com/f-secure-foundry/tamago/blob/60c33cfb0e60b29dda937a35cd11acd7a44acd42/soc/imx6/uart.go#L332-L338 waits for Tx FIFO to be empty after every character. Wouldn't that be better to wait before sending only if Tx FIFO gets full?

(I can't run it yet due to build issue https://github.com/f-secure-foundry/tamago-example/issues/8 )

title: Unable to get example working for RPi Zero
author: jphastings
body:
Hi folks,

I've had an [issue open on the RPi Zero example repo](https://github.com/kenbell/tamago-example-pizero/issues/2) for a month, but realised that may not be the right place to post this problem!

Building the example RPi Zero project ([with some tweaks, to fix the go.mod file](https://github.com/jphastings/tamago-example-pizero/tree/d478bd44f6462df42ff87adac1cbd9078798d21a)) creates a kernel file which builds successfully, but fails to run on any of my RPi Zeros.

[These are the files](https://github.com/f-secure-foundry/tamago/files/6396993/tamago-rpi0.zip) I've had on my SD card, but I get no flashing LED, no printed output and no clues I know how to follow as to further debugging (I also tried including `bcm2708-rpi-zero-w.dtb` as it's called out as missing in the UART log, with no luck).

How should I proceed in trying to fix this?
